name: Makar Sankranti
significance: The sun enters Capricorn, marking the end of winter solstice. A day of harvest, charity, and solar worship. Sacred dips in rivers wash away sins.
sadhanaNote: Offer water to the rising sun at dawn, chant the Surya Ashtottara 108 times, donate sesame and jaggery.

name: Vasant Panchami
significance: The fifth day of spring marks the arrival of Vasant Ritu. Saraswati — goddess of knowledge, music, and arts — is worshipped. Students seek her blessings for learning.
sadhanaNote: Dress in yellow, place books and instruments before Saraswati's image, chant her mantra 108 times, and begin new studies on this day.

name: Magh Purnima
significance: The full moon of Magh month. Bathing in sacred rivers is considered highly meritorious. Vishnu worship and charity are central practices.
sadhanaNote: Fast through the day, bathe at dawn, light deepa, chant Vishnu Sahasranama, and offer tulsi leaves.

name: Maha Shivratri
significance: The great night of Shiva. Most powerful night for Shiva sadhana, fasting, and all-night vigil. Shiva performed his cosmic dance of creation and dissolution on this night.
sadhanaNote: Fast through the day, chant Om Namah Shivaya 108 times each prahar of the night, perform abhisheka with milk and bael leaves.

name: Phalguna Purnima
significance: Full moon of Phalguna. Holika Dahan is performed this evening — the burning of Holika symbolising the destruction of evil and the victory of devotion.
sadhanaNote: Perform circumambulation around the Holika bonfire chanting Narasimha mantra. Fast until evening and break the fast after Holika Dahan.

name: Holi
significance: The festival of colours celebrating the divine love of Radha and Krishna, and the victory of devotion over arrogance. Spring's arrival is honoured with joy.
sadhanaNote: Chant Krishna's names at dawn before the colours begin. Meditate on the Vrindavan lila. Offer fresh spring flowers to Krishna.

name: Chaitra Navratri Begins
significance: The first day of Chaitra Navratri — nine nights of the goddess. Shailaputri is worshipped on the first day. The Shakti within all beings is awakened.
sadhanaNote: Begin a nine-day fast. Install the kalash, light the akhanda deepa, and chant Durga Saptashati daily. Offer red flowers.

name: Ram Navami
significance: The ninth day of Chaitra — the birth of Lord Rama. The ideal king, the seventh avatar of Vishnu. Reading the Ramayana and Ram Naam japa are the sacred practices.
sadhanaNote: Fast until midday (Rama's birth hour). Chant Ram Naam 108 times or recite Hanuman Chalisa. Offer tulsi, saffron, and sweets.

name: Chaitra Purnima
significance: Full moon of Chaitra. Hanuman Jayanti falls on this purnima — the birth of Lord Hanuman, the supreme devotee and embodiment of strength and service.
sadhanaNote: Read Hanuman Chalisa and Sundara Kanda. Offer sindoor and jasmine flowers. Fast and break with prasad.

name: Hanuman Jayanti
significance: The birth of Hanuman — the divine monkey god, Chiranjeevi and greatest servant of Rama. His worship grants strength, protection, and liberation from fear.
recommendedMantra: ॐ हं हनुमते रुद्रात्मकाय हुं फट्
transliteration: Om Ham Hanumate Rudratmakaya Hum Phat
sadhanaNote: Recite Hanuman Chalisa 11 times, apply sindoor tilak, offer boondi prasad. Visit Hanuman temple at sunrise.

name: Akshaya Tritiya
significance: The undecaying third — a day when any action bears imperishable fruit. Auspicious for new beginnings, charity, and gold. Treta Yuga began on this day.
sadhanaNote: Begin any new sadhana today — it will bear eternal fruit. Offer lotus flowers, chant Lakshmi mantra 108 times, donate food to the poor.

name: Vaishakha Purnima
significance: Full moon of Vaishakha. Also celebrated as Buddha Purnima — the birth, enlightenment, and parinirvana of Gautama Buddha. A triple-auspicious full moon.
sadhanaNote: Observe silence, meditate on the full moon, practice charity and compassion. Chant Vishnu Sahasranama and offer white flowers.

name: Buddha Purnima
significance: Triple sacred day marking Buddha's birth at Lumbini, enlightenment at Bodh Gaya, and Mahaparinirvana at Kushinagar. The most sacred day in the Buddhist calendar.
sadhanaNote: Practice noble silence, observe the five precepts, meditate for extended periods. Offer candles, flowers, and incense at the altar.

name: Jyeshtha Purnima
significance: Full moon of Jyeshtha. Vat Savitri Vrat is observed by married women for the longevity of their husbands, recreating Savitri's victory over Yama.
sadhanaNote: Worship the banyan tree, tie sacred thread around its trunk, chant Savitri Stotra, observe fast and break it at moonrise.

name: Guru Purnima
significance: The full moon of Ashadha — the most sacred day to honour the Guru. Sage Vyasa, the compiler of the Vedas, was born on this day. The Guru-Shishya parampara is celebrated.
sadhanaNote: Perform Guru Puja at dawn. Meditate on your guru's form. Chant the Guru Gita. Offer flower garlands and prostrate at the feet of your teacher.

name: Ashadha Purnima
significance: Full moon of Ashadha, also observed as Guru Purnima. The day Vyasa began teaching the Brahmasutras. Chaturmas — the four sacred months of spiritual intensification — begin.
sadhanaNote: Begin a Chaturmas vow — give up one comfort for four months. Do Guru Puja, chant 108 repetitions of Guru mantra at sunrise.

name: Nag Panchami
significance: Worship of the serpent deities who guard the earth's treasures and kundalini energy. Ananta Shesha, Vasuki, and Takshaka are honoured on this day.
sadhanaNote: Offer milk to anthills and snake images. Do not dig the earth today. Chant Naga mantra 108 times. Draw serpent symbols with turmeric at the entrance.

name: Shravana Purnima
significance: Full moon of Shravana — also Raksha Bandhan. The sacred thread of protection is tied between siblings. Rishis renew their sacred thread (Upakarma) on this day.
sadhanaNote: Tie raksha (protective thread) while chanting Rakshoghna mantra. Brahmins perform Upakarma and renew their sacred thread commitment.

name: Raksha Bandhan
significance: The sacred bond of protection between siblings. Lakshmi tied a thread to Bali to secure Vishnu's protection. The raksha symbolises divine protection and dharmic duty.
recommendedMantra: येन बद्धो बलि राजा दानवेन्द्रो महाबलः
transliteration: Yena Baddho Bali Raja Danavendra Mahabalah
sadhanaNote: Tie the raksha thread during the auspicious muhurta while chanting the Rakshoghna mantra. Light incense and offer sweets.

name: Janmashtami
significance: The birth of Lord Krishna — the eighth avatar of Vishnu — at the stroke of midnight. The cosmos rejoiced when the divine flute-player entered the world to restore dharma.
sadhanaNote: Fast through the day until midnight. Chant Krishna's 108 names as midnight approaches. At midnight, bathe the Krishna murti, offer butter and tulsi. Break fast with prasad.

name: Bhadrapada Purnima
significance: Full moon of Bhadrapada. Pitru Paksha begins after this full moon — fifteen days of ancestor worship, offerings to departed souls.
sadhanaNote: Perform tarpana (water offerings) to ancestors at dawn. Feed Brahmins and cows. Light sesame lamps to guide departed souls.

name: Ganesh Chaturthi
significance: The birthday of Ganesha — remover of obstacles, lord of beginnings, patron of intellect and wisdom. Celebrated for ten days with great joy and devotion.
sadhanaNote: Install Ganesha murti at your altar. Offer modak, durva grass, and red flowers. Chant Ganesha Atharvashirsha. Avoid looking at the moon on this night.

name: Sharad Navratri Begins
significance: The nine sacred nights of Sharad Navratri — the most celebrated of the four Navratris. Nine forms of the Divine Mother are worshipped over nine nights.
sadhanaNote: Install the Devi yantra on your altar. Read one chapter of Durga Saptashati daily. Maintain brahmacharya and pure diet. Light akhanda deepa for all nine nights.

name: Navratri Day 2 — Brahmacharini
significance: Second form of Durga — the one who walks the path of brahmacharya (austerity). Her worship grants merit, renunciation, and stability in sadhana.
sadhanaNote: Offer white flowers and sugar. Practice intensified silence and tapasya today. Read chapter 2 of Durga Saptashati.

name: Navratri Day 3 — Chandraghanta
significance: Third form — the goddess whose forehead bears a crescent moon bell. She rides a tiger and her sound drives away evil spirits and negative energy.
sadhanaNote: Offer milk sweets and white sandalwood. Ring a bell during puja to invoke her protective presence. Read chapter 3 of Durga Saptashati.

name: Navratri Day 4 — Kushmanda
significance: Fourth form — she who created the universe with her cosmic smile. Resides in the sun and bestows vitality, radiance, and health.
sadhanaNote: Offer malpua and orange flowers. Meditate on solar light entering your heart. Read chapters 4-6 of Durga Saptashati.

name: Navratri Day 5 — Skandamata
significance: Fifth form — the mother of Skanda (Kartikeya). She carries her son and the lotus, bestowing salvation and purifying the heart through motherly grace.
sadhanaNote: Offer bananas and yellow flowers. Meditate on divine motherhood. Read chapters 7-8 of Durga Saptashati.

name: Navratri Day 6 — Katyayani
significance: Sixth form — the warrior aspect born to slay Mahishasura. Her worship grants courage, the fulfilment of righteous desires, and removal of marital obstacles.
sadhanaNote: Offer honey and red flowers. Chant her mantra to overcome obstacles. Read chapters 9-10 of Durga Saptashati.

name: Navratri Day 7 — Kalaratri
significance: Seventh form — the dark, fierce night of time who destroys all ignorance and fear. Despite her terrifying appearance, she always bestows auspiciousness.
sadhanaNote: Offer jaggery and blue night-blooming flowers. Meditate on fearlessness. Read chapters 11-12 of Durga Saptashati.

name: Navratri Day 8 — Mahagauri
significance: Eighth form — the radiant white goddess who purifies all karma. Her complexion shines like the moon, and she wears white garments as a symbol of purity.
sadhanaNote: Offer coconut and white jasmine. Perform Kumari puja. Read chapter 13 of Durga Saptashati. Observe complete silence for one prahar.

name: Navratri Day 9 — Siddhidatri
significance: Ninth and final form — the bestower of all siddhis (supernatural powers). Seated on a lotus, she grants moksha to the sincere seeker who has completed all nine days.
sadhanaNote: Perform Navami Havan. Read the final chapter of Durga Saptashati. Perform Kanya Puja — feed nine young girls as forms of the goddess. Break the nine-day fast.

name: Dussehra
significance: Vijaya Dashami — the tenth day victory. Rama slew Ravana on this day. Symbolises the victory of dharma over adharma, truth over falsehood, light over darkness.
sadhanaNote: Chant Ramcharitmanas verses at dawn. Burn symbolic effigy of ego and vice. Perform Shastra Puja — honour your tools of dharmic work.

name: Ashwin Purnima
significance: Full moon of Ashwin. Sharad Purnima — the autumn full moon when the moon is believed to rain nectar (amrita). Kheer offered to the moonlight is considered medicinal.
sadhanaNote: Place kheer in moonlight overnight. Chant Vishnu Sahasranama under the full moon. Consume the charged kheer as prasad at dawn.

name: Karva Chauth
significance: The vrat of devoted wives — a moonrise fast performed for the long life of husbands. It embodies the ideal of Pativrata dharma and conjugal love.
sadhanaNote: Fast from moonrise to moonrise. Listen to the Karva Chauth katha. Sight the moon through a sieve and then the husband's face before breaking the fast.

name: Dhanteras
significance: The thirteenth day of Krishna Paksha of Kartik — Dhanvantari, the divine physician, emerged from the cosmic ocean with the pot of amrita. Yama's lamp (Yamadeepa) is lit to avert untimely death.
sadhanaNote: Light a deepa facing south for Yamadeepa. Buy gold or silver. Worship Dhanvantari and Lakshmi at sunset. Chant her mantra 108 times.

name: Diwali
significance: The festival of lights — Rama's return to Ayodhya after 14 years of exile. Lakshmi walks the earth on this night, entering only illuminated homes. The darkest night becomes the brightest.
sadhanaNote: Light 108 deepas of oil. Perform Lakshmi puja at pradosh kaal (dusk). Chant Lakshmi Stotram and Shri Sukta. Keep the main entrance clear and illuminate it fully.

name: Govardhan Puja
significance: Krishna lifted Govardhan Hill on his little finger for seven days to shelter Vrindavan from Indra's wrath. Annakut — a mountain of food — is offered to Krishna.
sadhanaNote: Prepare annakut offering with 56 food items (chhappan bhog). Circumambulate Govardhan Hill or a symbolic mound of cow dung. Chant Krishna's name.

name: Bhai Dooj
significance: Yama visited his sister Yamuna on this day. She welcomed him with a tilak, and he granted that brothers who receive tilak from their sisters on this day will be freed from Yama's bondage.
sadhanaNote: Sisters apply tilak to brothers' foreheads. Offer worship to Yamuna. Brothers gift their sisters and pledge protection. Chant Yama mantra for longevity.

name: Kartik Purnima
significance: The most sacred full moon — Dev Diwali. Vishnu wakes from his four-month Yoganidra. Bathing in sacred rivers and lighting lamps on the water bestows liberation.
sadhanaNote: Bathe in a sacred river before dawn. Float oil lamps on water. Visit Vishnu temple and witness the Devotthan Ekadashi puja. Fast and break at moonrise.

name: Vivah Panchami
significance: The divine wedding anniversary of Rama and Sita — the ideal marriage of dharma, love, and devotion. Celebrated with the recitation of Vivah Khanda from the Ramayana.
sadhanaNote: Read the Vivah Khanda of Ramcharitmanas. Perform sankalpa for a dharmic marriage or to bless an existing marriage. Offer red flowers to Rama and Sita.

name: Margashirsha Purnima
significance: Full moon of Margashirsha — the month Krishna declares 'I am' in the Bhagavad Gita. Dattatreya Jayanti falls on this full moon — birth of the Adi Guru.
sadhanaNote: Read Avadhuta Gita or Dattatreya Upanishad. Offer three-colour flowers symbolising Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. Do Tripada Vibhuti sadhana.

name: Pausha Purnima
significance: Full moon of Pausha — the last full moon before Makar Sankranti. Bathing in sacred rivers and charity are paramount. The Kumbh Mela cycle is honoured.
sadhanaNote: Bathe at dawn, perform charitable acts, chant Vishnu Sahasranama, and light a deepa. Begin preparation for Makar Sankranti.

name: Phalguna Ekadashi
significance: Amalaki Ekadashi — the most sacred Ekadashi of the year. The amalaki tree (Indian gooseberry) is Vishnu's favourite. Worship of this tree on this day bestows liberation.
sadhanaNote: Fast completely on Ekadashi. Worship the amalaki tree with offerings. Read Ekadashi Mahatmya. Break fast on Dwadashi with tulsi water.

name: Chaitra Ekadashi
significance: Papamochani Ekadashi — the remover of all sins. A sage's son was freed from a curse of being turned into a demon when he observed this Ekadashi with devotion.
sadhanaNote: Fast and chant Vishnu's names throughout. Read Papamochani Ekadashi Katha. Break fast with prasad of tulsi and fruits on Dwadashi.

name: Vaishakha Ekadashi
significance: Mohini Ekadashi — when Vishnu appeared as Mohini to distribute amrita. One of the most powerful Ekadashis for liberation from the cycle of rebirth.
sadhanaNote: Observe nirjala (waterless) fast if possible. Chant Vishnu Sahasranama and read Bhagavata Purana. Offer tulsi garland to Vishnu.

name: Nirjala Ekadashi
significance: The most powerful Ekadashi of the year — one who observes this complete waterless fast gains the merit of all 24 Ekadashis. Bhima (of the Pandavas) observed this.
sadhanaNote: Observe nirjala (no water) fast for the full 24 hours. Chant Vishnu Sahasranama continuously. Donate water, umbrellas and fans to Brahmins. Break fast with Panchamrit.

name: Ashadha Ekadashi
significance: Devshayani Ekadashi — the day Lord Vishnu enters yogic sleep (Yoganidra) for four months. The four sacred months of Chaturmas begin. Pilgrimage and austerities are paramount.
sadhanaNote: Begin a Chaturmas vow today. Observe fast, chant Vishnu Sahasranama. Take resolve for four months of intensified spiritual practice.

name: Shravana Ekadashi
significance: Putrada Ekadashi — the giver of sons and virtuous progeny. Couples desiring children observe this Ekadashi with special devotion and Vishnu worship.
sadhanaNote: Fast and perform Vishnu puja. Read Putrada Ekadashi Katha. Donate food to children. Chant Vishnu Sahasranama with sankalpa for progeny.

name: Bhadrapada Ekadashi
significance: Aja Ekadashi — the destroyer of all sins. Even reciting its name removes the sin of Brahminicide, according to the Brahma Purana.
sadhanaNote: Fast and chant the thousand names of Vishnu. Read Aja Ekadashi Mahatmya. Donate sesame seeds. Meditate on Vishnu's form throughout the night.

name: Ashwin Ekadashi
significance: Indira Ekadashi — the liberator of ancestors. Performing this Ekadashi vrat releases ancestors from lower planes and elevates them to Vishnu's realm.
sadhanaNote: During Pitru Paksha this Ekadashi is especially potent. Offer tarpana to ancestors, fast, and chant Vishnu Sahasranama for their liberation.

name: Kartik Ekadashi
significance: Devotthan Ekadashi — Lord Vishnu awakens from Yoganidra on this day ending Chaturmas. Marriage ceremonies resume. The most auspicious Ekadashi for Vaishnavas.
sadhanaNote: Wake before dawn and perform abhisheka to Vishnu's murti. Chant Vishnu Sahasranama. Tulsi Vivah is performed — the sacred marriage of Tulsi to Shaligrama.

name: Margashirsha Ekadashi
significance: Mokshada Ekadashi — the granter of liberation. Krishna narrated the Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna on this day. Reading the Gita bestows the same merit as all Ekadashis combined.
sadhanaNote: Read the complete Bhagavad Gita. This is Gita Jayanti — the most sacred day to begin a Gita study practice. Fast, meditate on Krishna's words, chant Vishnu Sahasranama.
